breaking.text: VIX spikes to 22.08, highest since autumn 2025, signaling elevated fear
S&P 500 cracks 100-day moving average, closes near 6,820 as 2026 gains evaporate
Iran claims missile strike on U.S. oil tanker in Persian Gulf; WTI crude surges past $79
Broadcom beats Q1 estimates with record $19.3B revenue; AI chip revenue doubles to $8.4B
Oil surges 8%+ this week after Iranian IRGC Navy strikes American oil tanker in Persian Gulf

summary: U.S. markets suffered another bruising session on March 5 as the S&P 500 fell roughly 0.8% to close near 6,820 after decisively failing to breach 6,900 resistance, with year-to-date gains now fully erased. The selloff was driven by surging crude oil prices—WTI spiked above $79 per barrel after Iran claimed to have struck an American oil tanker near the Strait of Hormuz—stoking inflation fears and pushing the VIX to 22.08. Eight of eleven S&P sectors ended lower, with defensive rotation accelerating as Microsoft dropped 17% YTD and airlines faced downgrades on fuel cost concerns. Investors now turn to Friday's February nonfarm payrolls report amid a Fed stance shifting toward a data-dependent pause with no rate relief expected before June.

highlights.text: The Trade Desk surged 19% on reports of a potential ad partnership with OpenAI, while Tango Therapeutics jumped 25% on pancreatic cancer drug trial news
February nonfarm payrolls report due Friday morning — key labor market test as Fed signals no rate relief before June amid core CPI running 2.5-3.1%
Broadcom earnings in focus as chip giant reports solid results; CIENA drops 16.5% on tepid 2026 outlook despite 76% cloud revenue growth
S&P 500 cracked its 100-day moving average at 6,835, closing near 6,820 with 2026 gains fully erased and next support at the 200-day MA near 6,570-6,590
WTI crude surged past $79/barrel after Iran claimed a missile strike on a U.S. oil tanker near the Strait of Hormuz, threatening 20% of global oil exports
Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang says $30B OpenAI investment 'might be the last time' before startup's potential IPO later this year
February nonfarm payrolls expected at just 50K as Kaiser Permanente strike affecting 31K workers weighs on healthcare hiring
American Airlines downgraded to neutral by Rothschild as surging jet fuel costs push EPS forecasts negative for 2026
Iran's IRGC Navy strikes American oil tanker near Strait of Hormuz, sending crude oil above $79 and triggering biggest weekly gain since 2022
Broadcom Q1 revenue hits record $19.3B (+29% YoY), AI chip revenue doubles to $8.4B; CEO sees path to $100B AI chip revenue by 2027

summary_history.text: The S&P 500 closed near 6,820 on Thursday, down 0.56%, decisively failing the 6,900 resistance level and erasing all 2026 gains as a triple threat of sticky inflation, hawkish Fed policy, and Middle East escalation rattled investors. Oil prices surged over 8% after Iran's Revolutionary Guard Navy struck an American oil tanker in the Persian Gulf, sending crude above $79 and triggering the commodity's biggest weekly gain since 2022. After the bell, Broadcom delivered a blowout Q1 with record revenue of $19.3 billion and AI semiconductor sales doubling year-over-year to $8.4 billion, sending shares up 5% in extended trading. Markets now brace for Friday's February jobs report, where economists expect a modest 50,000 payroll gain weighed down by a Kaiser Permanente strike that affected 31,000 workers during the BLS survey week.
